summary: My name is Kwame Wood, also known as Rasskwame. I was born in Accra, Ghana in 1963 and have been passionate about music since I was a child. After moving to the United States in 1990 to tour with C.K. Mann, I lived in New York City, Washington, D.C., and Minneapolis, where I formed bands and recorded albums. Music is my life’s work, and I compose, arrange, and produce my own songs, focusing on themes like uplifting the oppressed and honoring my ancestors.

I was born in Accra, Ghana in September 1963. Growing up there, I developed a love for music early on and joined a student band that won a national contest in 1977. Accra is where my musical journey began before I eventually moved to the United States in 1990 to pursue my career further.

Ghana is my homeland where I was born and raised, specifically in Accra. It’s where I first started playing music and met the famous musician C.K. Mann in 1986. Although I moved to the United States in 1990, I have had the chance to visit Ghana twice since then, and my music often pays homage to my Ghanaian roots and African heritage.

I came to the United States in 1990 to join C.K. Mann on a North American tour, but due to visa delays, I ended up staying here. I lived in New York City, then Washington, D.C., where I founded my band Culture Shock, and later moved to Minneapolis in 1997. In 2010, I became a U.S. citizen, and despite the challenges of being an independent artist here, I continue to pursue my passion for music.

I met C.K. Mann, a famous Ghanaian musician, in 1986 during one of my band's performances. He invited me to join him on a North American tour in 1990, which was my initial reason for coming to the United States. Although the tour didn’t go as planned due to visa issues, that connection was pivotal in starting my life and career here.

After arriving in New York City in 1990, I faced a culture shock but adapted by driving a taxi and joining a band there. I spent about five years in New York before relocating to Washington, D.C. It was an important period for me to establish myself musically in the United States.

In Washington, D.C., I founded my own band called Culture Shock and recorded my first album, Ancestors, in 1997. This city was crucial for my growth as an independent artist and bandleader. It was also where I met my kids' mother before moving to Minneapolis.

Culture Shock is the band I founded in Washington, D.C. after moving from New York City. It was during this time that I recorded my first album, Ancestors, in 1997. The band was my way of expressing my musical vision and connecting with audiences through reggae and world beat.

I moved to Minneapolis in 1997 after meeting my kids' mother, who is from there. We have three children together, and it’s where I formed the Ananse Band. Minneapolis has been my home since then, and I recorded my second and third albums, Ananse Stories and True Experience, while living here.

The Ananse Band is the group I formed after relocating to Minneapolis in 1997. This band allowed me to continue creating music that reflects my African heritage and personal experiences. With them, I recorded my second album, Ananse Stories, in 2010, which carries themes important to my culture and storytelling tradition.

Ancestors was my first album, recorded in Washington, D.C. in 1997. This album reflects my deep respect for my heritage and the people who came before me. It was an important milestone in my career as an independent artist in the United States.

Ananse Stories is my second album, which I recorded in 2010 while living in Minneapolis. The album draws on African folklore and storytelling traditions, especially the Ananse tales, to share cultural messages through music. It represents my ongoing commitment to honoring my roots and educating others through my art.

True Experience is my third album, released in 2015. It continues the themes of my previous work, focusing on uplifting the oppressed and unifying the African continent. This album reflects my growth as a composer and producer, and my dedication to independent music-making in the United States.

Reggae is the primary genre of music I play, blending it with world beat influences. My music uses reggae rhythms to deliver messages about social justice, honoring ancestors, and African unity. This style has been central to my identity as a musician since I started performing professionally.
